speaker: interviewer
text: Tape Number two, September 28, 1995. The survivor is Regina Gaska. We were talking about the ghetto, people dying like flies, being covered with newspaper.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw this a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in the ghetto? You were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I couldn't do nothing. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Peeling potatoes, or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was day-to-day life like in the ghetto for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Waiting for then what's going to be tomorrow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was no life in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never worked. Nothing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We sold anything we got of value. We sold it for a piece of bread, or for something to survive. The Polish people came over the fence, and we exchanged things like a piece of jewelery, or something. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay inside most of the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed inside, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people you were living with, was anybody getting sick, all these people in one apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my mother got sick in the ghetto. She got typhus in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a hospital? What happened when your mother got sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I did help her, wash her face in cold water, the fever. And my sister got sick in the same time. Nothing you could do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they both get better?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I got sick after this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got a crazy typhus. I was screaming at night and all day. And they had to lock me in because they were afraid I'll say something, and the Germans catch on to it. I was screaming [? hell ?] of my head off. And I was pulling my skin from my body off. And then I started to get better. After 15, 16 days, you get better from the typhus. And you cannot walk. I started to learn to walk again. You lose your hair from the typhus, became like a skeleton. Then I got better. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then we got-- they made Judenrein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, before that, though, were there Jewish leaders in the ghetto? What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a few Jewish leaders in the ghetto. The men in there, they call it the Sonderkommando. They took the dead, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] picked them up and buried them in the cemeteries. There's not many leaders. Some police, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Jewish leaders treat the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them nice and some of them-- usually, you know, what could they do? Nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After 8 o'clock, you couldn't walk in the street anyway. 8:00 or 6:00, I don't remember. They were so strict that you cannot walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any incidents happening with the German soldiers? Did you have contact with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You're afraid to look at them. You're scared to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see things happening that they did to other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We see them, sure. Cutting the religious Jews' beards off, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make any attempt at keeping the religious calendar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they kept something. There was nothing to keep, really, at that time. It was Passover, but it's not Passover. We didn't have matzoh. We didn't have anything, so there was nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any sort of school at all? Was anybody tutoring anybody? Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any radios? Did anybody have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --No knowledge. You knew nothing about what was happening outside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you outside the ghetto at all once you went in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only time they went out of the ghetto, when they took us out in the town to the trains. That's the only time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went into the ghetto in 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what time of year it was when you went into the ghetto? Was it around Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Passover, we were in the ghetto. This, I remember. I was in the ghetto. There was nothing to eat and nothing, anything. We stayed in a room, a [INAUDIBLE] of this room, in a little room. We knew the people who were living there, they weren't there no more. They were shot in the bed. Somebody told us, a neighbor.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, where was this where people were shot in bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the round-ups start? What did you know about that? As the ghetto got smaller and smaller?

speaker: interviewee
text: Smaller, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us they're going to a camp. They'll take us to a camp, and we're going to work, and they're going to have bread for us, and things like that. You wouldn't have hunger. You didn't have this. You're going to be all right. The people were happy already to get out of this kind of life. We took some belongings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you remember about the ghetto? Any other incident that you want to mention or discuss?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were so many things. It's hard to remember this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, any-- I mean, you said that they--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot of shooting in the ghettos. Plus, I was in the ghetto shootings. Was in the ghetto shootings, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep each other together, your mother and her sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just sat like chickens on the floor and looked at my mother all the month of April.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have a strong belief in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she didn't talk too much about God. She said just one thing. With six children, she says, I have two. I hope they don't take them away from me. That's the only two words she said the last time. I mean, this kind of hurt she left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were taken the end of 1942, right? What was it like? What do you remember about the day when they told you you were-- how much notice did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: No notice. They ran around the ghetto, and they told us to come out, and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came out with your mother and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then? Where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take us to a place close to the trains. They checked us out from top to bottom.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they check you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: When, for instance, you had a watch on, or you've got a piece of jewelry with you, they took you into the barrack-- it was a big barrack-- and they checked you. They took the clothes off of you. They ripped the clothes off of you. And they examined you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a man or a woman who examined you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A man.

speaker: interviewer
text: How thorough an examination?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thorough. And I got beaten. I came out. I couldn't even recognize nobody. My mother did not recognize me, because they find a piece of jewelry on me. And I didn't give him up the jewelry. I kept it. So I got beaten. I got checked all over places.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came out. Even a German came over, and he says, who beat you so much? I says, I don't know. I wasn't allowed then to say who did it to me. I couldn't walk. I couldn't do anything. I got so beaten up. My back was all blue and swollen.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was crying, and that's all. It was then they took us to the trains. The train was so crowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the train look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train doesn't look like normal. Like the train you travel cows in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They wasn't trains for human beings. And it was so crowded. The people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know many cars there were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how many cars. The cars where we were were so crowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Do you know how many people they took that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: No idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there dogs and guns? What did the Germans do to get you on the trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dogs and guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you got on the train, and it was very, very crowded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Crowded, you couldn't even turn around. We were laying-- there was a little spot within the trains, that we breathe in a little air. I was already asleep. My mother woke me up. Most of them fall asleep right away. We were stepping on them like garbage, on the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing

speaker: interviewer
text: --a window?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no windows. There was the-- what do you call this-- perspiration from the ceiling was dripping off us on everybody. Everybody just fell asleep like, sweet sleep. People became so blue. Their heads were swollen. From the whole train, came out about six, seven people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around how many were on the train that came out in the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot of them in the train,

speaker: interviewer
text: On your particular car, were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Several dozen? Several--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: More?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe five people. And my friends from school was in that train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I mean before, how many people, around, were on the train? And you said only five, six, seven came out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a lot of people on the trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: I really can't tell you because I don't know. There was a lot of people on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about sanitary conditions on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not sanitary conditions. People made their way, they said, they made some.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the train stop at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food at all, the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how long the trip was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: A couple days?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: No idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about when you got to the camp? Was it daytime or nighttime?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daytime, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember hearing and seeing? You said that, at this point, many on the people on the train were dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you, your mother, and your sister were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The man came-- a man, a German, made right, left, right, left. I was with my sister, and they took away my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What camp was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Majdanek.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember seeing when you got out of the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were so doped up, I think, we didn't see anything. I just was holding onto my mother and to my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the last thing your mother said to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what she said to us, I thought God wouldn't punish me so much. He's going to take this away from me too? We kissed, and that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's brother was already in camp. And I ordered-- but the wires, he was talking to me. I says, well, did you see Ma? He says, no. And with the way he said no, I know that something is wrong. I said to my uncle, why can't you see Ma and tell me how she's doing? He said, I'll try. That's all. There was barbed wires already made. He was before, a few months in Majdanek. What happened when you first got to the camp? What was the process like of the registration?

speaker: interviewee
text: They enclosed us, took the clothes off. They gave us their own clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the clothes like?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is the clothes? KL marked. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was red marks, KL.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Kayel'?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: K-L?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Concentration camp. And that's all. They gave us clothes, and they sent us the next to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the barrack? Where were you staying?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in a barracks in Majdanek. The barracks wasn't so bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many girls were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I slept with my sister. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How many? I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are a lot of girls with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Young girls. All young. In Majdanek, I met my cousins. They came before us. And they said to us, is that you, [? Gina? ?] I says, yes. She looks so swollen, blown up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says to my sister, what's wrong here? They were blown up from hunger. When you put a needle, the water would explode. That's how big they were. They didn't live long. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Majdanek was a death camp. What happened when you first got there, that day? You said that they put you to work right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work  were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Majdanek, we did in the gardens, vegetables picked for the Germans. Lettuce, carrots. That's all. Every day, we worked like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. Then we worked for brick, the Jewish houses, to take them apart, the bricks separating, build the road from the bricks. We helped them to take care of the-- what do you call it? They put the bricks in-- what would you call it there when you put the bricks like yet in a-- it's not a car. It's a wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You pull the-- the bricks stayed in a special place. They built the roads. Majdanek wasn't build the roads. We had to build the roads. So is in Auschwitz too.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Majdanek, what was the daily routine like for you? Was there a roll call?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is the daily routine?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Work?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, was there a roll call where everybody-- appel? Everybody--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, say appel. Yeah, sure. Sure, you have to. Every morning, they count you. And every night, they count and say appel. You have to stay, and they counted them over till everybody showed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days a week were you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day. No, Sunday, we didn't work. I think Saturdays we didn't work. Otherwise, we worked every day. I don't remember about Saturdays, funny.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say it was a death camp, what do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: People dying like flies. There was a kapo, she used to give them 25 on the behind. When we're not standing straight, or we're not holding the [INAUDIBLE] right, or there's something, you got slapped, a beating, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the kapo, or any names of any of the guards, or kapos, or anything? What happened to you while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I tried not to get in their way. I just tried to be quiet, stay away from them. Whatever they told us to do, we did it. That's all. This is the way you learn to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of food did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rations. Water and something, water with something mixed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember the food, even, in Majdanek, what we ate. I don't really remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you hungry all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Hunger pains, a lot. A lot of hunger pains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people fight with each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nothing to fight. Matter of fact, we got along nice with them. Strange people, we slept together. We got along nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: People you've kept in contact with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm still in contact with some of them from when I got liberated, yes. Some of them in Israel. One is in Milwaukee. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We still wrote letters to each other. Especially New Year's, we send cards to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the women there stop getting their periods, the women in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no periods in the camp. Nobody had periods in the camp. This was a blessing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we got fever, too, but you had to go to work. It's no excuse, fever or no fever. My friend from Milwaukee, she got something-- what do you call it? I forgot. Something. She got a sickness. She got the chills. I forgot what they used to call it. And we had to protect her. Otherwise, they would take her away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you protect her?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the kapo came in when we were working, I said, Dineh, stand up. She's here. Marco is here. We gave her a name. Marco is here. You have to sit up. You cannot lay no more. We had to hold on and pretend she's exchanging the work. I forgot the name of the sickness. Malaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Malaria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She got a terrible malaria. She was sick like a dog.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any medical attention there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you needed to go to the bathroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to hold on. Someone went with you. You didn't go by yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times a day would they let you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when  you were on camp, not at work, they let you go, because there was no place there to escape anyway. So you went by yourself, because always, you're allowed to go. So you went.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were at work, it was different?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I really don't remember what happened at work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was everybody in the camp Jewish, all the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In the camp, we were all Jews, yeah. They make selections every day, every other day, they came. It get smaller. The people disappeared from the places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. At that time, we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they going to Auschwitz? Did you know where they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were in Majdanek a couple months and went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what did you know about this selection that they were doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew it already.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we knew it in camp, yeah. They're talking already. When we came, people were there already, so they told us that everything is a lie. They don't give you anything. It's a death. And you have to be strong, and maybe you'll make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think when you heard all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a way, I said to myself, I'm glad my sister's not there, the oldest. Because I got a message she's still alive. So they did press us, the family outside. See about a relative's house that's living. You could write letters. They told us all kind of things. I says, I have nobody there. I have no letters to write.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people get mail?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't get mail. They wanted to find out what kind of Jews are hiding. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You're supposed to send out the mail. I'm supposed to send out the mail to my relatives, so they could catch them and bring them into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that that's what they were doing when they asked you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The people told us, don't give any information. You know somebody's alive in the family, don't write. Don't tell them anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody keep track of Jewish holidays, or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the camp, yes. In Auschwitz, they did. And from Greek-- you know? What do you call it? Greek is-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Greek is what country? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some Arab country Jews-- why do you call it? Greek? Greek it would be-- [INAUDIBLE]. What is this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll talk about Auschwitz in a little while.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they are very religious. I don't know how they find a candle, and they told us it's holidays. They knew everything. I don't know how. And they told us it's holidays. And we have to pray. They prayed, and when they would catch us, there wouldn't be prayers no more. We kept quiet, Jews. We laid like everyone would be dead, crying by himself in there. Yeah, but they knew something. We didn't really know anything. It didn't come from under the Jews that there was something. When Sunday came, we didn't work. We knew it  was Sunday. Otherwise, we didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever punished for anything in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't punished, no. We stayed, say appel by everybody. When somebody else did wrong, we got punished too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any incidents when somebody did something wrong, what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: When somebody did wrong-- somebody didn't show up to say appel. They didn't have to do much wrong. They didn't show up just.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would they do to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed for hours outside in the cold. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Made us stay for a nice couple of hours' punishment. Or they made the ends up or the kneeing down by the fence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you remember about Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any guards who were nice to you or particularly cruel?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed away from them. We didn't get involved with none of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was responsible for discipline?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the kapos on the block. There were women kapos on the block. They took responsible, [GERMAN]. You take care of the cleaning on the block. They were responsible. We did something wrong, they punished us. They took away the bread-- that's a big punishment-- the sliced bread from the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you took care of your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took care of my sister, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she get sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: She got very sick, yes. She got terrible pains in her stomach.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you try to do for her when she was sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried to go to the kapo to give me something, some water to drink. She said nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was no pity. Well, I couldn't do nothing for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel helpless. What could I say? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She got such terrible pains, hunger pains, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at Majdanek for?

speaker: interviewee
text: About three months, or four months, something like that. And they took us to-- they told us that we're going to a different camp. But then we found out they're supposed to send us to gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew you were going to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. They told us that we're going to another camp. But they put us in a-- what do you call it? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They put us someplace, and we saw something on the ceiling, like gas would come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, well, was your sister with you when you went to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My sister was taken away in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before, in Majdanek, were you both taken by the cattle cars again to Auschwitz? How did you get to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Something cattle-- I think cattle cars? I don't remember, really. I really don't remember. We came together to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see when you got there? Was it daytime or nighttime?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was  in the daytime, early morning. We were all black. They were from coals, the trains, the cars. And we came out all black, that the German, said, what did you bring me some garbage here? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He said the strong, the healthy, they'll do work. So they took us to showers, gave us a shower.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what was the selection process? Did you see any signs, or anything, when you got to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Arbeit macht frei.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: Work will let you free. When you work, you'll get free.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you got there, you were with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: The selection was there to older people, younger people. There was over-selection. They took pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans. After they took us out from the shower, they got pictures. They cut our hair off, completely bald, anyplace. Sterilize us like--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, sterilized you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They get all kind of junk on the body. You shouldn't have germs, or something. And then they let us lay on the-- night and a day, I think, we were laying without blankets in the camp. They sterilized everything. You shouldn't be no germs, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was it when you got there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it summer, spring? What was the weather like outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was springtime.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still with my sister, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in the barracks with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we slept together too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the daily routine here? What were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the clothes. Whatever they gave us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this clothes like? Different from what you had in Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was clothes from the transport which Jews, they'd bring into the camps. We didn't wear the [INAUDIBLE]. We wore regular clothes they gave us. One was long, and one was short-sleeve. One was a right shoes, and one was a left shoes. They gave you clothes in the camp, in Auschwitz.
